metadata.scope: DORA (Regulation EU 2022/2554) and EU AI Act (Regulation EU 2024/1689)

well_covered.title: Detection
well_covered.requirement: Financial entities must establish mechanisms to promptly detect anomalous activities including ICT network issues and incidents, and identify potential critical failure points.
well_covered.rationale: Multiple controls address real-time detection, anomaly monitoring, and incident classification through SIEM, monitoring dashboards, and continuous risk assessment.

partially_covered.title: Identification
partially_covered.requirement: Financial entities must identify, classify and adequately document all ICT supported business functions, roles, information assets, and ICT assets; conduct annual reviews and assess risks.
partially_covered.rationale: RC-1A provides risk classification. GC-0B provides end-to-end traceability. However, gaps exist in initial ICT asset inventory, continuous vulnerability scanning, and change impact analysis.
gaps: Initial ICT asset inventory and documentation

partially_covered.title: General requirements for digital operational resilience testing
partially_covered.requirement: Financial entities must create and maintain a comprehensive digital operational resilience testing programme including risk-based assessments, independent testing, and validation methods.
partially_covered.rationale: SAST and DAST address testing methodologies. Infrastructure security covers environment validation. Gap: no explicit control for independent testing verification, penetration testing coordination, or test result trending.

poorly_covered.title: Governance and organisation
poorly_covered.requirement: Management body must maintain governance framework, define ICT risk policies, approve strategy and risk tolerance levels, oversee business continuity plans, and establish reporting channels.
poorly_covered.rationale: RC-5A (CAB Approval) addresses formal governance approval but does not cover management body accountability, risk tolerance definition, or ongoing governance oversight.
gaps: ICT risk appetite and tolerance level definition

poorly_covered.title: ICT-related incident management process
poorly_covered.requirement: Financial entities shall define, establish and implement an ICT incident management process including documentation, root cause analysis, consistent monitoring, and follow-up.
poorly_covered.rationale: SC-6A provides incident detection and classification but does not address root cause analysis procedures, corrective action tracking, or incident process governance.

poorly_covered.title: Classification of ICT-related incidents and cyber threats
poorly_covered.requirement: Financial entities must categorise ICT incidents per DORA taxonomy assessing impact through six criteria and classify significant cyber threats.
poorly_covered.rationale: SC-6A detects and logs incidents but single control insufficient for comprehensive DORA classification framework with six impact criteria.

poorly_covered.title: Reporting of major ICT-related incidents
poorly_covered.requirement: Financial entities must report major ICT incidents to competent authorities with specific timelines: 4h initial, 72h intermediate, 1 month final, plus client notification.
poorly_covered.rationale: SC-6A detects incidents and GC-0A provides audit trail, but no control explicitly manages DORA reporting timelines, regulatory notification, or client communication procedures.

poorly_covered.title: Testing of ICT tools and systems
poorly_covered.requirement: Testing must include vulnerability assessments, open source analysis, network security assessments, gap analyses, physical security reviews, source code reviews, scenario tests, compatibility testing, and penetration testing.
poorly_covered.rationale: DAST (SC-4B) covers dynamic testing but does not address the full range of DORA test types including open source analysis, network assessments, physical security, or scenario-based testing.

poorly_covered.title: General principles of sound management of ICT third-party risk
poorly_covered.requirement: Financial entities must manage third-party ICT risk, maintain registers, conduct due diligence, ensure compliance, implement contracts, and develop exit strategies.
poorly_covered.rationale: GC-0C provides agent provenance but does not address third-party vendor management, contract requirements, concentration risk assessment, or exit strategy validation.

not_covered.title: Protection and prevention
not_covered.requirement: Financial entities must continuously monitor ICT systems, deploy appropriate security tools, establish security policies, implement access controls, deploy strong authentication, implement change management, and maintain patch policies.
not_covered.rationale: While individual security controls exist (SC-*), they are not explicitly mapped to Art. 9. This represents a significant gap in documenting comprehensive protection mechanisms.
gaps: Authentication and access control policy documentation
Data protection at rest, in transit, and in use

not_covered.title: Response and recovery
not_covered.requirement: Financial entities must implement ICT business continuity policy ensuring continuity of critical functions, limit damage through recovery procedures, test plans annually, and maintain crisis management function.
not_covered.rationale: RC-5B validates rollback plans but no control explicitly addresses business continuity policy, crisis management, or cross-functional recovery procedures.

well_covered.title: Record-keeping
well_covered.requirement: High-risk AI systems shall technically allow for automatic recording of events (logs) over their lifetime with traceability of system functioning including period of use, reference databases, and input data summaries.
well_covered.rationale: Multiple governance controls provide comprehensive logging, traceability, and audit trail capabilities meeting AI Act record-keeping requirements.

partially_covered.title: Risk management system
partially_covered.requirement: A continuous iterative risk management system must identify known and foreseeable risks, estimate and evaluate risks, evaluate post-market monitoring data, and adopt targeted risk management measures.
partially_covered.rationale: AC-1A provides AI risk classification and RC-4A evaluates risk thresholds. AC-6A addresses post-market monitoring. Gap: no explicit control for iterative risk assessment process or foreseeable misuse scenarios.
gaps: Risk acceptance and residual risk documentation

partially_covered.title: Reporting of serious incidents
partially_covered.requirement: Providers must report serious AI incidents to market surveillance authorities within 15 days (10 days if death involved, 2 days for widespread infringements), with detailed incident information.
partially_covered.rationale: AC-6A provides post-market surveillance and SC-6A detects incidents but neither explicitly manages AI-specific reporting timelines or serious incident classification.
gaps: Serious incident definition for AI systems
Regulatory reporting workflow for AI incidents

poorly_covered.title: Classification rules for high-risk AI systems
poorly_covered.requirement: AI systems are classified as high-risk if they are safety components of products under Union harmonisation legislation or fall within Annex III categories (biometrics, critical infrastructure, employment, etc.).
poorly_covered.rationale: AC-1A provides AI risk tier classification but does not explicitly address Annex III categories or product harmonisation legislation mapping.
gaps: Annex III category identification and mapping

poorly_covered.title: Transparency and provision of information to deployers
poorly_covered.requirement: AI systems must be accompanied by instructions for use with concise, complete information including provider details, system characteristics, capabilities, limitations, intended purpose, accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity, potential risks, and human oversight measures.
poorly_covered.rationale: AC-2A provides design review but does not explicitly ensure transparency documentation for deployers or instructions for use preparation.

poorly_covered.title: Human oversight
poorly_covered.requirement: High-risk AI systems must be designed with human-machine interfaces enabling effective human oversight during use, with oversight assigned to competent, trained natural persons with authority and support.
poorly_covered.rationale: AC-2A addresses design review and SC-2B (directives) provides guidance injection but does not address human oversight competency, training requirements, or organizational assignment.

poorly_covered.title: Post-market monitoring
poorly_covered.requirement: Providers must actively collect and analyze data on performance and compliance of AI systems throughout their lifetime; post-market monitoring plan must be proportionate to technology risks and included in technical documentation.
poorly_covered.rationale: AC-6A provides post-market surveillance but single control insufficient for comprehensive monitoring plan, data collection methodology, and performance analysis framework.

poorly_covered.title: High-risk AI systems (biometrics, critical infrastructure, employment, etc.)
poorly_covered.requirement: Lists specific AI system categories requiring compliance with Chapter III Section 2 requirements including biometric identification, critical infrastructure management, employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration, and justice.
poorly_covered.rationale: AC-1A classification control does not explicitly address each Annex III category or ensure category-specific compliance requirements are triggered.
gaps: Conflict resolution (EU AI Act vs. sectoral law)

not_covered.title: Data and data governance
not_covered.requirement: High-risk AI systems must use training, validation, and testing data meeting quality criteria; governance must address data collection, preparation, bias examination, and data gap identification.
not_covered.rationale: AC-4A addresses bias testing but no control explicitly manages data governance, data quality criteria, or data preparation processes.

not_covered.title: Technical documentation
not_covered.requirement: Technical documentation for high-risk AI systems must be prepared before market placement with minimum elements specified in Annex IV, including system description, development process, monitoring, risk management, and changes throughout lifecycle.
not_covered.rationale: QC-4C addresses documentation completeness but does not explicitly reference AI Act technical documentation requirements or Annex IV elements.

not_covered.title: Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity
not_covered.requirement: High-risk AI systems must achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity; be resilient against attempts to alter use or outputs; protect against adversarial attacks, data poisoning, model poisoning, and adversarial examples.
not_covered.rationale: AC-4A addresses bias and fairness but no control explicitly addresses accuracy metrics, robustness requirements, or adversarial attack resilience.

not_covered.title: Quality management system
not_covered.requirement: Providers must implement a documented quality management system ensuring regulatory compliance with specific elements: strategy, design control, development procedures, testing procedures, technical specifications, data management, risk management, post-market monitoring, incident reporting, communication protocols, record-keeping, resource management, and accountability framework.
not_covered.rationale: Multiple controls address individual elements but no control explicitly implements a comprehensive QMS per AI Act Art. 17(1) requirements.
gaps: Resource management and supply chain security

not_covered.title: Obligations of deployers of high-risk AI systems
not_covered.requirement: Deployers must use AI systems in accordance with instructions, assign oversight to competent personnel, ensure input data quality and representativeness, monitor system operation, report issues to providers, and immediately notify authorities of serious incidents.
not_covered.rationale: GC-0C addresses agent provenance but no control explicitly addresses deployer obligations, oversight assignment, or input data validation.
gaps: Issue and incident reporting to provider

not_covered.title: Technical documentation elements for high-risk AI systems
not_covered.requirement: Specifies minimum elements of technical documentation including general description, detailed element description, monitoring and control information, risk management system description, lifecycle changes, harmonised standards, and EU declaration of conformity.
not_covered.rationale: QC-4C addresses documentation completeness but does not explicitly implement Annex IV element requirements.

feedback_loop_gaps.description: Analysis of how feedback loops address regulatory requirements when issues are detected in Stage 4 or Stage 6

path_a_coverage.name: Path A — Quick Fix → Stage 3
regulatory_requirements_covered: Art. 9(2) - Code integrity and malicious code detection
Art. 15(3) - Security analysis and integrity verification
gaps: No explicit human review/approval for expedited fixes
Limited to low-risk issues only; no medium/high risk feedback coverage
Does not address root cause analysis or architectural issues

path_b_coverage.name: Path B — Full Re-entry → Stage 1
regulatory_requirements_covered: Full DORA and EU AI Act coverage through complete stage re-entry
Art. 9 - Complete protection and prevention review
gaps: No specific guidance on multi-stage re-entry for complex issues
Assumes all Stage 1-6 controls equally applicable to all issue types

strengths: Path A provides expedited remediation for low-risk issues while maintaining critical security controls
Path B ensures full regulatory compliance for complex issues through complete lifecycle re-entry
Both paths include GC-3A (code provenance) ensuring accountability
weaknesses: Gap in medium-risk feedback handling between Path A and B
No explicit regulatory reporting trigger in feedback loops for DORA/AI Act incidents
Feedback loop decision tree does not reference regulatory classification (DORA Art. 18)
No explicit third-party impact assessment in feedback loops (Art. 28)
critical_gaps: Feedback loops do not address DORA Art. 19 incident reporting timelines
No explicit mapping of feedback loop triggers to DORA/AI Act incident definitions
Missing control for escalation authority in feedback loops

overall_coverage.assessment: FRAMEWORK DEMONSTRATES FOUNDATIONAL COVERAGE BUT SIGNIFICANT REGULATORY GAPS EXIST

top_priorities: Art. 9 (Protection and prevention) - CRITICAL: No explicit controls despite being core DORA requirement
Art. 11 (Response and recovery) - CRITICAL: No business continuity policy or crisis management controls
Art. 5 (Governance) - HIGH: Management body accountability and risk tolerance not explicitly addressed
Art. 28 (Third-party risk) - HIGH: Vendor management and exit strategy controls missing
Art. 10 (Data governance) - CRITICAL: No controls for data quality, bias assessment, or data preparation
Art. 11 (Technical documentation) - CRITICAL: Annex IV elements not implemented despite QC-4C
Art. 15 (Accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity) - CRITICAL: No accuracy metrics, robustness testing, or adversarial attack resilience
Art. 17 (Quality management system) - CRITICAL: Comprehensive QMS not documented despite individual controls
Art. 26 (Deployer obligations) - CRITICAL: Input data validation and deployer oversight not addressed

critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.title: Protection and prevention
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.impact: Core DORA obligation for continuous monitoring and security tool deployment entirely unmapped
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.recommendation: Create SC-series controls for: ICT monitoring policy, security baseline, authentication framework, patch management, network segmentation

critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.title: Response and recovery
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.impact: No business continuity, crisis management, or RTO/RPO controls; single control for rollback insufficient
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.recommendation: Create RC-series controls for: Business continuity policy, BIA, RTO/RPO definition, crisis management function, BC testing

critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.title: Data and data governance
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.impact: No data governance controls despite being foundational for high-risk AI systems
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.recommendation: Create AC-series controls for: Data governance framework, data quality criteria, bias assessment, data preparation, gap analysis

critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.title: Technical documentation
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.impact: Annex IV requirements not explicitly implemented; QC-4C insufficient for AI Act documentation scope
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.recommendation: Enhance QC-4C or create new AC control explicitly implementing all Annex IV elements with AI-specific documentation requirements

critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.title: Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.impact: No controls for accuracy metrics, robustness testing, or adversarial attack resilience; AC-4A (bias) alone insufficient
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.recommendation: Create AC-series controls for: Accuracy metric definition, robustness testing, adversarial attack simulation, model poisoning defense

critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.title: Quality management system
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.impact: Comprehensive QMS requirement not implemented as unified control; individual elements scattered across tracks
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.recommendation: Create new GC control consolidating QMS requirements or enhance existing control to explicitly implement all Art. 17(1) elements

critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.title: Incident reporting and regulatory timelines
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.impact: No controls explicitly manage DORA (4h/72h/1mo) or AI Act (15/10/2 days) reporting timelines
critical_gaps_requiring_immediate_action.recommendation: Create SC-series control for incident reporting workflow with automated timeline tracking and regulatory notification

phase_1_critical_gaps: SC-9A: ICT Monitoring and Detection Policy Implementation
RC-7A: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery
AC-5A: AI Model Robustness and Accuracy Testing
SC-7A: Incident Reporting and Regulatory Notification
phase_2_coverage_improvements: Enhance Art. 5 governance controls with management body accountability
Enhance Art. 28 third-party controls with vendor management and exit strategy
Create comprehensive technical documentation control for Art. 11/Annex IV
Create deployer obligations control for Art. 26
phase_3_integration: Map feedback loops to regulatory incident classification
Implement regulatory timeline automation in incident response
Create cross-functional controls for Art. 28 third-party coordination
Add regulatory reporting artifacts to control outputs

QC (Quality Controls).gaps: Art. 11 (business continuity), AIACT Art. 10 (data governance), AIACT Art. 11 (documentation completeness)

AC (AI Controls).gaps: AIACT Art. 10 (data governance), AIACT Art. 15 (accuracy/robustness), AIACT Art. 26 (deployer obligations)

GC (Governance Controls).gaps: DORA Art. 5 (management body), AIACT Art. 17 (comprehensive QMS)
